speaker: interviewer
text: You were describing-- you were crossing the border to get to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Into a DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- did you know that you were going for a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what you were looking for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I was supposed to go to Munich, according-- what we read on the walls-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the dead telephones. We found names that are cousins of ours out in Munich. So we planned to go to Munich. As we crossed the Czechoslovakian-German border, we were trying to walk and see if we can find any communication to have a ride wherever we want to go, to the nearest station to get a train or a bus. So we found a truck with wood-- a German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we asked him where is he going? He said, I'm going to Deggendorf. We didn't know Deggendorf. We didn't have any idea where Deggendorf was. But he told us that there's a railroad station in Deggendorf. And that's the way we planned to do to come into Munich, how to get there. So he took us on the truck on the road. We're hungry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he drives. It took him an hour or maybe less to come into Deggendorf.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the Austrian-- if you look from that mountain from the station, Eisenstein-- Eisenstadt was the station there that-- we went through the border right on the station. From one side, you could see the Alps into Czechoslovakia, into Austria. So the, the corner was Bavaria, Germany, Austria, and Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says he's going to Deggendorf. So, OK. So we're taking a ride, and we're coming in Deggendorf. And he stopped in the middle of the center town, across from city hall.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- this is here. We're getting off. And we're standing, like, looking around. We're, we're from here. So we're here. By-passers going down. People passing, they talk Yiddish. And we stopped them. The name was Kelman. I remember now the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we, we ask him in Yiddish, [YIDDISH]. He says, yeah, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. You know, our, our slogan after the war, to find out if anybody's a Jew-- we couldn't go over to a Polack, saying, you a Jew? So we said, [YIDDISH]. So a Jew knew what [YIDDISH] is. [YIDDISH] is a Jew, you know. So they says, yes. So they say, where are you going? He says, from where are you? I said, I'm from Poland. From where? From Bedzin. Where are you going? To Munich. Why do you have to go to Munich? That-- we have in here a DP camp, Displaced Persons. And we have in here a man from Bedzin. He is on the committee. And then another man which, by coincidence, was a brother-in-law to my brother-in-law, to my sister's husband-- a brother-in-law. So we went, and we walked into that camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that-- my sister's brother-in-law brought me a, a dish of butter, two pounds of butter, with the [INAUDIBLE]. But you took sick after that-- eating them. I was so hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it looked like we were trying to settle in that DP camp, 1945. So we settled in. And a matter of fact, I have a paper from that camp-- 45 years. As long as we-- the first Yiddish Hanukkah after the war, in that camp, we were celebrating.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to show you those things. I get it out for you. It's a document, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we met already. We had one gathering from the DP camp in San Diego about six year, seven years ago-- from that DP camp from Deggendorf. Now we're going to meet in here. Probably tomorrow night or Saturday night, we'll have a dinner together, not too many people. But we'll find out tomorrow. We were going to get together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we settled in DP camp. There's-- my wife had two brothers in Canada, not knowing an address, but knowing a name-- an aunt, mother's sister-- sister of her mother by the name Rose-- two brothers Holender. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So the UNRRA-- the United Nation Refugee Administration, they were called, after the war, the UNRRA. So they had Jewish people. A lot of Jewish people get involved in uniforms. So I remember one of-- a chaplain. An army chaplain-- worked for the UNRRA-- came in. And I remember I gave him an address. It was Jasper Avenue in, Alberta. I told him, Jasper 'Av-eh.' We didn't know 'avenue.' So he took the house and Jasper Avenue-- he took it. And sooner or later, we had a letter from Canada. Two brothers [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] are there and the aunt. Much help, we didn't get. But at least we got in contact. You found out where they were. Did you ever find out anything about the whereabouts of your mother and stepfather and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I came home--

speaker: interviewer
text: --sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came home to Bedzin-- we had also a committee in Bedzin when I came home for furlough before I was yet discharged. And everybody went to that committee and also put the names down. So if anybody decided to leave Bedzin like I left, we left our names behind. If anybody will come later, or anybody came before us, which-- I couldn't locate [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nobody from my family. Which-- actually, before the war broke out, we didn't live in Bedzin. We lived in another city, six kilometers, which the city was Dabrowa. I mentioned that to you, Dabrowa. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I gave you another address. I think, now, [NON-ENGLISH] 33, in Dabrowa. And I didn't go to Dabrowa. I don't know for what reason, but it was very scary. Dabrowa didn't have many Jews before the war-- not as Bedzin. It was a Jewish city. So to go to Dabrowa was scary. I, I didn't trust, too much, the Poles. Being in the army, which I will turn a little bit back, which gets-- memories come back. I was afraid for my Polish officers-- very anti-Semitic. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As much they could hurt, they did. Because me-- they knew that I'm a Jew. I wouldn't-- I wasn't hiding anything to be Jewish. And matter of fact, we have a sanitary officer in our battalion. He was Jewish. He was a surgeon already. If we were a few Jews, we kept our-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we stick together, you know. We both kept-- and, like, if we went out, if they go-- went Sunday to church, the soldiers. We were trying to seek if it was-- where is a synagogue to see. But it was all closed up. Going-- the Russians closed up. The Germans destroyed. The Russians didn't believe. Communistic Poland, anti-Semites, they took it over for certain things for their benefits.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't have much to go to worship. But we kept together as Jews, you know. We kept close. Did you personally ever experience anything from the others because of your being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot. A lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of things happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I lived in-- we lived in bunkers at the first line. And sometimes, I was threatening-- was threatened to be-- with certain things as being a Jew, you know. Some-- excuse me to express myself, which I shouldn't-- but some dumb Polack [LAUGHS] expressed his name-- his, his, his attitude. He showed his attitude.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- I have pictures, which I-- a few of them. I remember [NON-ENGLISH] and, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]. A few names I can recall. But it's-- I had no interests. They weren't mine-- associates. I remember more the Jewish names, that the surgeon was Sherman. One I had from not far from my city, Blok. And I said, as my cousin, by coincidence, was with me. He's in Brazil now, in Sao Paulo, you know. And the one I, I, I took away from the camp-- I told you, I changed his uniform. He's in Brazil now. The three brothers are in Brazil now.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you keep from getting hurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't know why. I was liked by people. I was very much liked by people. I have to tell you a story. Coming back to Siberia, that there was a woman doctor, a Jewish doctor. Not a Jewish-- a woman doctor that she took care of this. And she, she liked me very much. When I walked into the office, she said, you stay home tomorrow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was lucky to those things, you know. I was very likable by people. You know, I was raised that way. As much as I was trying to be foxy to resist, I had to use sense how to do it, you know? You cannot be two things.

speaker: interviewee
text: When it comes to fight for your life, you've got to show who you are. And if it comes to live with people, you have to know how to-- it. And if I was always hunted by somebody, I, I didn't resist. In the army, to show mine-- arrogant-- that I am-- I leave it be. So he cooled off the other way. It was-- he called me dirty Jew. And I had, had to live with that. It was like a breakfast over lunch. 'Dirty Jew' wasn't-- for them nothing that-- something that I didn't know that they-- that they knew any difference. This is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think was the most dangerous experience that you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being in the first line with the Polacks. I never gave mine-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sergeant a chance to be behind me. I never trusted him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He is right in the picture that I'll show it to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any other experiences that took place during the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you'd like to share?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- what can I tell you? Wherever I was-- I was, before I was sent into Siberia to a camp, I was three months in a cell by myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And every night, 1 o'clock in the morning, 2 o'clock, I was taken out and taken in to a KBG man, NKVD, for interrogation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That I should commit myself-- I was a capitalist. And I was a contre-revolutionnaire. And I was slapped in my face by the Russian KBG a few times, also called [SPEAKING RUSSIAN], which is, you dirty, sloppy Jew. And we could have never believed that they were fighting for a free world, communism-- that we will experience so much heartaches by them. And I made a slogan about the Russians. They don't let you live, but they don't let you die. So this was our experience to find out who we are and with whom we are among-- what kind of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you learn to speak Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I was in the camp in Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: You, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was young. I was young. I, I was writing Russian. I can still write. I can still read Russian. I, I forgot-- I cannot express myself as fluently.

speaker: interviewer
text: I used to speak when I was 50 years younger, you know? If you don't practice, if you don't speak-- but I will read a letter. And I will understand the language very well. What other languages do you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I speak the English language, Yiddish, Polish, German. And I do get into Hebrew, but I don't speak Hebrew well. I never had the chance to-- Hebrew doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to the DP camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at Deggendorf. We left off there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind--

speaker: interviewee
text: --settled in in the DP camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And every camp had a police force, but not, you know, with ammunition, with guns-- security, for security reasons. And, as I was a-- an army man discharged from an army, they were looking for it. And I was already in Germany and in the camp. I was recorded into the Berihah-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] into the Berihah. We smuggled out children from Germany into Italy, and then to Palestine--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Jewish. I was recorded. And I was on the police. I went on night duty. I got to come to that. I'll tell you what happened. We decided, me and Anni, to get married. We lived in one room, two sisters and myself. And I had a dream. My mother came to me. I told you, it was-- and she told me, it's not right to live with women like that. It's not our belief-- in a dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I talked to Anni. And I decided, are we getting married? If not, you go your way, I go my way, I says.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, two girls and-- with me together in a home. They had their bed. I had my bed. And the camp treated us very well, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we get a-- February, we got married. And the same day in February, through the-- through the UNRRA we're notified about this, that we are going to get married in Canada. So the same day of the wedding, a woman officer from the UNRRA, a Jewish woman officer, brought us a telegram from Canada wishing mazel tov.

speaker: interviewee
text: February the 3rd, we got married in camp. And we had a wedding. It was four weddings in the same camp every week. You know, young people came out from the camps. And mine was the biggest wedding, had over 200 people, with a German band from eight people playing all night. We had friends, which a few-- living in Israel. When I was in Israel in 1988, I met one of the friends, sitting over there and eating dinner with us. And one made schnapps. People baked. We slaughtered a cow. And we, we had a wedding. One in our men, who was giving us away, taking me to the chuppah, me, and his wife, they're living in Los Angeles. And their daughter is a, a judge in juvenile court. She was carrying my aunt's veil, you know, like, the train-- she was holding. And this is good, good memories to remind ourselves. And after we got married, when I was in the police, we settled already in. And Mali, the sister, used to travel a little bit to her sisters. We went on our honeymoon to another DP camp to those cousins that we found on the wall. One of-- they came to our wedding. And we went with them to their camp. They were in the Hashomer Hatzair to Fohrenwald, behind Munich. And this was our honeymoon, together in a room for 20 people. It's all right. That's all we-- listen, we were young, understanding. I was always aware of myself and my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we came back-- so when I was on the police, at nighttime, which my-- Anni never knew that I had carried a gun with me. She never saw it because she wouldn't have let me, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we smuggled-- we got together children. Already was lists, you know, what, and whom. And we took, always five, six, children, two men. One was already driving. I didn't have any license, but the other one-- and we drove them up to the mountains, which Deggendorf was the place for it up into the-- same place we came down. And we smuggled them into Austria and to Italy. And somebody took it over, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was told that I would not be able to emigrate to Canada so quick because I was on the English blacklist. Because England didn't like us to go to Palestine those years, you know. They were very much against. And what, they sent back the and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I adjusted myself for the United States. And Mali, the sister-- her brothers were trying to take  her the whole time. She was already a minor, you know, yet, not even-- about 18-- close to 18. Anni was 19 when she married. And every Sunday, Mali went to a-- and I worked in, in, in Germany for a little while. And then, when I start to go to the CIC for interviewing, was a American officer from the CIC. But he was Polish descent. And he spoke Polish to us. Because they sent people that-- they can talk the languages because we couldn't talk any English. And when he looked at my birth certificate, and he's-- watched that I was born  in Paris, he said, you don't have to wait too long for your visa because you're a French citizen. And which-- it didn't take long. I wasn't even interrogated because I was a French citizen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were allowed to bring your wife with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my wife-- were already married. We had already all the papers. So it didn't take long, and-- till the end of '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do where you worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the police. In--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were paid for that? That was a salary?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we got paid. That, that-- it was like-- how do you call the, the money in the army? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like coupons, let's say, was camp coupons for money. We had a canteen. You could buy things. But we had food for nothing, you know. We had a kitchen. We had-- supported by the Joint Distribution Center, you know, the Joint, the HIAS.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Jewish, Jewish-- the Jewish community in the world woke up, but too late, you know. Did the American Jews carry a conscience about the Holocaust? They woke up too late. But blame, yes, or blame, no, it wasn't the news-- newsreel system wasn't explored as, as, as much as it is today. There was no television.

speaker: interviewee
text: A matter of fact, from the underground, when we got already communication with any other units in this forest, we knew a man of the name John [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH], [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] John [NON-ENGLISH]. He was very good for Jews. And he was a courier from Europe to England, from England to the United States. He was a Polack. But he was in favor of us Jews-- Jewish people. And he came in here to this country to the president of the United States-- Roosevelt. Those years, we had a, a Jew sitting on the Supreme Court, Felix Frankfurter. And he confronted the both, both of them. And he told them about the atrocities, what the Germans are doing. So he was told, my dear friend, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we're not calling you a liar. But we don't believe you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, a German Jew, Frankfurter-- German Jew, huh? Jews idealized Hitler to begin with. Because they thought that whatever Hitler will do, he will never touch them-- very naive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So the, the, the story goes back and forth. Everything is for survival. If I have a thing we're telling you, we paid a price. I paid a price with my family. My wife paid a price with her family. And everyone of us paid a very dear price. Did you ever find out about the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I try-- I was trying. But till now, I never heard a response-- till now.

speaker: interviewer
text: None of the sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And not your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. My mother, she couldn't survive. I think she, she went. My older sister, she didn't want to give up her four children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some people had the choice-- give up your children and go in the labor camp. And it was still no guarantee for survival, you know. You go, you go. But she didn't want to give up her children. So she had to go with the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother-in-law survived, which-- I met him in Germany. After already I got married, I found out he is in Landsberg [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a DP camp. Landsberg is a town where Hitler was in prison. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was when Hitler was as a prisoner. Before he became the leader of Germany, he was a few years in a prison. That's where he wrote his

speaker: interviewer
text: So I met my brother-in-law. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went to see him. He already had a-- with him a woman. She was already pregnant. Some people didn't use their sense.

speaker: interviewee
text: I talked to him. He never mentioned to me my sister. I could never get out-- maybe he was afraid to talk in front of his-- well, finally, he went to Paris. He had brothers in Paris before the war. From Paris, he went back to Poland. He had children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Which they thought that Poland will be-- I tried to escape from Poland. They will try to go back to Poland. And-- but then Poland, in 1968, when they chased the Jews out for good, then he ran to Israel. And for him it was too late to survive, make a life. And he didn't live long. His wife is alive with children from him. And I never-- seeing her. I never had the chance to get together with her. But I wondered when a man had four children, and he never mentioned to me anything. So I didn't want to go into-- force and get out of him things that-- I left. And I've never seen them since then.
